findings.finding: Wrapping blocking calls in asyncio.to_thread moves them to threadpool workers, where CPython delivers signals only to main thread — making blocking syscalls less signal-responsive after the R29 wrap, not more. Task 16 has no test for partial-completion / signal-mid-handler behavior.
findings.evidence_quote: every blocking call ... inside their bodies is preceded by await asyncio.to_thread(...)

findings.finding: psutil children(recursive=True) finds PGID-divergent grandchildren only while intermediate parents are alive. Workers in the runner own PGID receive the same SIGTERM and may exit before the runner handler finishes enumeration; reparented grandchildren disappear from the runner descendant tree.

CORTEX_ALLOW_INSTALL_DURING_RUN=1 bypass produces split-brain escalations under in-flight upgrade — orchestrator-agent writes (re-rendered prompt → per-session) diverge from in-memory OLD-code feature_executor.py / outcome_router.py writes (still repo-level), silently degrading cycle detection.
findings.reviewer_angle: Escalations cleanup cutover hazard via install-guard bypasses (Tasks 10 ↔ 17)

findings.finding: CORTEX_ALLOW_INSTALL_DURING_RUN=1 leaks via env-inheritance into runner-spawned subprocess children. subprocess.Popen calls in runner.py do not pass env=. A user who sets the bypass to recover from a stuck session and later starts a new run silently re-disables R28 protection.
findings.reviewer_angle: Pre-install guard import-time scope and bypass fragility (Task 17)

findings.finding: Guard has no liveness check on active-session pointer — checks phase \!= complete only, no verify_runner_pid call. A crashed runner leaves a phase: executing pointer that locks out all future cortex invocations until user finds and runs the cancel-bypass incantation.
findings.reviewer_angle: Pre-install guard import-time scope and bypass fragility (Task 17)
findings.evidence_quote: aborts when ~/.local/share/overnight-sessions/active-session.json exists with a phase \!= complete field
